title: Cyclobalanopsis gracilis
intro: Cyclobalanopsis gracilis, also known as the Taiwan chinquapin, is a species of deciduous tree in the beech family Fagaceae. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which have been shown to help protect against cardiovascular disease and reduce the risk of certain cancers. The bioactive constituents of Cyclobalanopsis gracilis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are thought to be responsible for its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to make various herbal preparations, including teas, infusions, and extracts, which are used to treat a range of health conditions, including digestive issues and fever.
taxonomy: Cyclobalanopsis gracilis is classified as a species of tree in the family Fagaceae, commonly known as the beech family. It is a type of oak tree native to East Asia. Specifically, it belongs to the genus Cyclobalanopsis and the section Cyclobalanopsis, which includes several other species of oak trees. The classification of Cyclobalanopsis gracilis is further supported by its morphological characteristics and genetic analysis. Its classification within the Fagaceae family highlights its evolutionary relationship with other beech and oak species.
uses: Cyclobalanopsis gracilis, also known as the Korean chestnut, has been traditionally used in Eastern medicine for its potential health benefits. According to some studies, Cyclobalanopsis gracilis may have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which could help to alleviate symptoms of certain health conditions. In traditional Chinese medicine, Cyclobalanopsis gracilis is often used to treat conditions such as fever and digestive issues.

constituents: Cyclobalanopsis gracilis contains a range of bioactive compounds, including phenolic acids, flavonoids, and terpenoids. These active constituents are responsible for the plant's medicinal properties and antioxidant activities. The flavonoids present in Cyclobalanopsis gracilis, such as quercetin and kaempferol, have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory and anti-cancer activities.
